perturbation.original_text: "Confidential Information" means the confidential or proprietary technical or business information of a party, including without limitation (a) proposals or research related to possible new products or services; (b) financial statements and other financial information; (c) reporting information; (d) the material terms of this Agreement and the relationship between the parties; and (e) planned launch dates. All of the information will be considered confidential only if it is conspicuously designated as "Confidential," or if provided orally, identified at the time of disclosure as confidential. "Confidential Information" does not include information that (i) is in or enters the public domain without breach of this Agreement, (ii) the receiving party lawfully receives from a third party without restriction on disclosure and without breach of a nondisclosure obligation, or (iii) the receiving party knew prior to receiving such information from the disclosing party or develops independently.
perturbation.changed_text: "Confidential Information" means any information related to this agreement. All information, regardless of whether it is marked confidential or not, falls under the definition of confidential information. "Confidential Information" includes information that (i) is in or enters the public domain without breach of this Agreement, (ii) the receiving party lawfully receives from a third party without restriction on disclosure and without breach of a nondisclosure obligation, or (iii) the receiving party knew prior to receiving such information from the disclosing party or develops independently.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that information must be designated as "Confidential" to be considered such, and stating that *any* information related to the agreement is confidential, the definition is broadened beyond typical legal interpretations and could conflict with trade secret laws, which often require active measures to maintain secrecy. This can create uncertainty regarding what information is truly protected and what obligations exist.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA and similar state laws define trade secrets as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. By defining all information related to the agreement as confidential, regardless of whether efforts are made to maintain secrecy or whether it is publicly available, the contract contradicts this definition and could jeopardize trade secret protection for legitimately confidential information. If something is in the public domain, it cannot be a trade secret, regardless of contractual language.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include 'any information related to this agreement,' regardless of whether it is marked confidential or whether efforts are made to maintain its secrecy. This directly contradicts the principles of trade secret law, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The Economic Espionage Act snippet (Snippet 1) shows various sections related to economic espionage which indirectly emphasizes the need to take measures to protect information. The altered text undermines the ability to claim trade secret protection, as it doesn't align with the standard legal interpretation that information needs to be actively protected to be considered confidential.

perturbation.original_text: "Subscriber" means any individual who purchases mobile content programming and: (a) who actually pays the applicable Subscription Fee; and (b) does not receive a refund of such Subscription Fee for any reason within thirty (30) days from the end of the payment period for which the Subscription Fee was paid. A Subscriber remains a "Subscriber" for the purposes of this Agreement so long as such individual continues to pay the applicable Subscription Fee.
perturbation.changed_text: "Subscriber" means any individual who initiates the download process for the programming. A Subscriber remains a "Subscriber" for the purposes of this Agreement until Distributor revokes this status, at its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: By defining 'Subscriber' as someone who *initiates* the download process, rather than someone who pays and doesn't receive a refund, the definition contradicts the typical understanding and legal interpretation of 'subscriber' in the context of paid services. This creates uncertainty regarding revenue sharing and obligations to paying customers.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification potentially contradicts Section 5 of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. § 45), which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. By defining a 'Subscriber' as someone who merely initiates a download, the distributor could unfairly represent the size of its user base, misrepresent the value of the service, and engage in deceptive billing practices, all of which could be considered unfair or deceptive acts affecting commerce.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Trade Commission Act | Federal Trade Commission Skip to main content The .gov means it’s official. Federal government websites often end in .gov or .mil. Before sharing sensitive information, make sure you’re on a federal government site. The site is secure. The https:// ensures that you are connecting to the official website and that any information you provide is encrypted and transmitted securely. Español Report Fraud Get Consumer Alerts Search the Legal Library Federal Trade Commission Act Tags: Consumer Protection Competition Appliances Alcohol Automobiles Clothing and Textiles Finance Franchises, Business Opportunities, and Investments Funerals Jewelry Real Estate and Mortgages Tobacco Advertising and Marketing Children Endorsements, Influencers, and Reviews Environmental Marketing Health Claims Made in USA Online Advertising and Marketing Telemarketing Advertising and Marketing Basics Credit and Finance Credit and Loans Debt Debt Collection Mortgages Payments and Billing Privacy and Security Children's Privacy Consumer Privacy Credit Reporting Data Security Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Red Flags Rule Mission Competition Consumer Protection Law 15 U.S.C. §§ 41-58, as amended Links https://www.ftc.govhttp://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml Files File Safe Web Act (194.69 KB) The Federal Trade Commission Act is the primary statute of the Commission. Under this Act, as amended, the Commission is empowered, among other things, to (a) prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce; (b) seek monetary redress and other relief for conduct injurious to consumers; (c) prescribe rules defining with specificity acts or practices that are unfair or deceptive, and establishing requirements designed to prevent such acts or practices; (d) gather and compile information and conduct investigations relating to the organization, business, practices, and management of entities engaged in commerce; and (e) make reports and legislative recommendations to Congress and the public. A number of other statutes listed here are enforced under the FTC Act. Return to top

perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Subscriber' as someone who merely 'initiates the download process' rather than someone who pays, contradicting the established understanding of what constitutes a subscriber in a paid service. This directly undermines the FTC Act's prohibition of 'unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce,' as highlighted in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which empowers the FTC to 'prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce.' By misrepresenting who a 'Subscriber' is, the company could inflate user numbers and engage in deceptive billing, thus creating a clear contradiction with consumer protection laws.

perturbation.original_text: 5.  OWNERSHIP. 5.1 By IMNTV. As between Distributor and IMNTV, IMNTV owns or has the necessary licenses to all copyright, trademark, patent and other intellectual property rights in and to, and all other right, title and interest in and to the Programming, the Trademarks and the IMNTV Portal. Except as expressly provided herein, IMNTV retains the right to distribute the Programming in any medium now known or hereafter developed. As between IMNTV and Distributor (excluding the Programming and other Provider intellectual property), IMNTV owns all copyright, trademark, patent and other intellectual property rights therein, and all other right, title and interest in and to or associated with the Subscription Products, the IMNTV Sites, the IMNTV Marks, and the System and Subscriber data.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. OWNERSHIP. 5.1 By IMNTV. As between Distributor and IMNTV, IMNTV is assumed to own or have licenses to all copyright, trademark, patent and other intellectual property rights. The ownership is assumed unless Distributor presents compelling evidence to the contrary. As between IMNTV and Distributor (excluding the Programming and other Provider intellectual property), IMNTV owns all copyright, trademark, patent and other intellectual property rights therein, and all other right, title and interest in and to or associated with the Subscription Products, the IMNTV Sites, the IMNTV Marks, and the System and Subscriber data.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'IMNTV is assumed to own or have licenses...unless Distributor presents compelling evidence to the contrary', the clause undermines established intellectual property law, which generally requires the owner to demonstrate ownership, not the other party to disprove it. This shift in burden of proof introduces uncertainty and could conflict with copyright and trademark laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S. Code § 201 outlines the ownership of copyright. The original language aligned with this, stating IMNTV owns or has licenses. However, shifting the burden of proof to the Distributor to disprove IMNTV's ownership challenges this. Copyright ownership is generally established through registration or creation, not by default assumption.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Chapter 2 - Circular 92 | U.S. Copyright Office Home Copyright Law of the United States (Title 17) Chapter 2 Chapter 2: Copyright Ownership and Transfer PDF Version (94 KB) Back to top 201. Ownership of copyright 1 (a) Initial Ownership.—Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.—In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.—Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106 , may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. Ownership of copyright (a) Initial Ownership .—Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire .—In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works .—Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106, may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts copyright law by shifting the burden of proof of ownership. The original text states that IMNTV 'owns or has the necessary licenses,' aligning with standard copyright principles. The change introduces an assumption of ownership unless disproven by the Distributor. This conflicts with 17 U.S. Code § 201, as presented in both scraped snippets, which states that copyright 'vests initially in the author or authors of the work.' The altered text effectively reverses this, requiring the Distributor to prove IMNTV *doesn't* own the copyright, which deviates from the legal requirement for the copyright holder to establish their ownership.
